caption: Figure 1: Illustration of the three sampling designs and their effects on spatial prediction. Columns correspond to random, clustered, and preferentially biased sampling for one represen- tative simulation setting (strong trend, ρ = 0.1). The top row shows the simulated response field on D = [0, 1]2 together with sampled locations. The second and third rows show the corre- sponding prediction maps obtained from random forest and heteroskedastic regression–kriging, respectively.
description2: The figure illustrates three sampling designs: random, clustered, and preferentially biased, demonstrating their effects on spatial prediction through simulated response fields and prediction maps for a strong trend setting with ρ = 0.1.
description3: Four-panel plot displays simulated response fields, random forest predictions, and heteroskedastic regression–kriging predictions for random, clustered, and biased sampling.
questions: How do different sampling approaches influence the resulting prediction maps?
What patterns are visually apparent in the simulated field and its predictions?

caption: Figure 2: Predicted annual mean NO2 concentrations across Germany obtained with random forest (left) and regression–kriging models (right), along with the locations of 503 monitoring stations. Both models use the same set of topographic and demographic covariates.
description2: Predicted annual mean NO2 concentrations across Germany are plotted, with random forest and regression-kriging models displayed on the left and right, respectively.
description3: Three-panel map displays predicted annual mean NO2 concentrations across Germany, using random forest and regression–kriging models, with 503 monitoring station locations marked.
questions: What factors were used to predict NO2 concentrations in this study?
How do the models represent predicted NO2 concentrations geographically?

caption: Figure 3: Joint distribution of prediction tasks in (x1, d) space in the simulation study. Panels compare the deployment task distribution with validation tasks generated by LOOCV, random 10-fold CV, LOBOCV, and buffered LOOCV, separately for the three sampling designs. Here, x1 denotes the large-scale environmental gradient and d the nearest-neighbour prediction dis- tance to the corresponding training set.
description2: The figure displays a joint distribution of prediction tasks in (x1, d) space, comparing deployment task distributions with validation tasks generated by different methods for three sampling designs.
description3: Two-panel plots display heatmaps showing the joint distribution of prediction tasks in (x1, d) space, comparing different cross-validation methods and sampling designs.
questions: How do different validation methods affect the distribution of deployment tasks?
What is the relationship between the environmental gradient and prediction distance?

caption: Figure 4: Mean error of RMSE estimators in the simulation study for different validation schemes and model-based uncertainty estimators for one representative simulation scenario (strong trend, ρ = 0.1). Points show the mean difference between the estimated RMSE and the true deployment RMSE, with approximate 95% confidence intervals. The dashed line indicates unbiased estimation of deployment RMSE. Positive values indicate overestimation of prediction error. Refer to the Supplementary Materials for results of other simulation scenarios and esti- mators.
description2: The figure plots mean error of Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) estimators with approximate 95% confidence intervals, showing points representing the mean difference between estimated RMSE and true deployment RMSE, with error bars indicating unbiased estimation.
description3: The plot presents a multi-panel scatterplot displaying mean RMSE estimator error with confidence intervals across three sampling schemes and estimator types.
questions: How do validation schemes affect the magnitude of RMSE estimator error?
What is the relationship between estimator error and unbiased RMSE estimation?

caption: Figure 5: Empirical distribution of prediction tasks in the space spanned by population density and prediction distance d for the 2018 NO2 case study in Germany. Panels compare the de- ployment tasks on the 2 km target grid with validation tasks induced by LOOCV, random CV, spatial CV, kNNDM, and buffered LOOCV. Local population density is shown on the log(1+x) scale.
description2: The figure plots the empirical distribution of prediction tasks in a space defined by population density and prediction distance d for the 2018 NO2 case study in Germany.
description3: The multi-panel plot displays empirical distributions of prediction tasks, comparing deployment with various cross-validation methods.
questions: How does population density influence the distribution of prediction distances?
What empirical patterns emerge when considering population density and prediction distance?

caption: Figure 6: Estimated root mean squared prediction error (RMSE) for annual mean NO2 con- centrations in Germany obtained from different uncertainty estimators and prediction models. Shown are the model-based estimate, LOOCV, random CV, spatial CV, and buffered CV in unweighted, importance-weighted (IWCV), distance-weighted (DWCV), and target-weighted (TWCV) form.
description2: The figure plots estimated root mean squared prediction error for annual mean NO2 concentrations in Germany obtained from different uncertainty estimators and prediction models.
description3: Two-panel scatter plot displays RMSE values against a generic axis, using triangles for random forest and diamonds for regression-kriging models.
questions: How do different weighting methods affect the estimated RMSE of NO2 concentrations?
What general trends are observed between prediction models and RMSE values?

caption: Figure S7: Mean RMSE estimator error across simulation scenarios for the considered vali- dation and model-based uncertainty estimators. Points show the mean difference between the estimated RMSE and the true deployment RMSE over simulation replicates, with 95% confi- dence intervals. The dashed line indicates unbiased estimation of deployment RMSE. Across scenarios, Buffered TWCV/IWCV remain essentially unbiased for both RF and HRK.
description2: Figure S7 plots mean Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) estimator error across simulation scenarios with points showing the mean difference between estimated and true deployment RMSE, accompanied by 95% confidence intervals and a dashed line indicating unbiased estimation of deployment RMSE.
description3: Two-panel plot displays mean RMSE estimator error with 95% confidence intervals across simulation scenarios for validation and model-based estimators.
questions: What main idea or comparison does the figure present?
What overall structure or pattern is shown in the figure?

caption: Figure S8: Standard deviation of RMSE estimator error across simulation scenarios for the considered validation and model-based uncertainty estimators. Points show the mean difference between the estimated RMSE and the true deployment RMSE over simulation replicates, with 95% confidence intervals.
description2: Figure S8 plots standard deviation of RMSE estimator error across simulation scenarios with points showing mean difference in RMSE estimates and 95% confidence intervals displayed as error bars.
description3: The plot presents a multi-panel scatterplot displaying the standard deviation of RMSE estimator error across simulation scenarios, with 95% confidence intervals.
questions: What main idea or comparison does the figure present?
What overall structure or pattern is shown in the figure?

caption: Figure S9: Effects of the TWCV/DWCV/IWCV weighting scheme on the stability of estimators in different sampling designs for one simulation scenario (strong trend, ρ = 0.1). From top to bottom: Effective sample size fraction (ESS / n); upper-tail (95th percentile) balancing weight concentration; mean RMSE estimator error for random forest and regression–kriging. Smaller ESS / n and higher 95th percentile weight values (>> 1) indicate increasingly uneven weighting.
description2: The figure plots the effects of TWCV/DWCV/IWCV on estimator stability in different sampling designs, showing effective sample size fraction, upper-tail balancing weight concentration, and mean RMSE error for random forest and regression-kriging.
description3: Three-panel plot displays box plots of effective sample size fraction, percentile weight, and mean RMSE error across three sampling designs.
questions: How do different sampling designs influence estimator stability?
What patterns emerge in the upper-tail balancing weight concentration?

caption: Figure S10: Non-stationarity of the NO2 air-quality data: Empirical and fitted semivari- ograms of linear-model residuals for urban locations (population density > 1000 km−2) and rural locations; overall semivariogram for comparison. Semivariograms are the geostatistical parameterization of spatial dependence of spatial random fields.
description2: The figure displays empirical and fitted semivariograms of linear-model residuals for urban and rural NO2 air-quality data, with an overall semivariogram for comparison.
description3: Two-panel plot displays empirical and modeled semivariograms of linear-model residuals for urban (red triangles), rural (blue down triangles), and overall (black circles) locations.
questions: How does air quality vary spatially between urban and rural locations?
What is the general trend of spatial dependence for air quality in urban versus rural areas?

caption: Figure S11: Effective sample size fraction (ESS / n) and upper-tail (95th percentile) balancing weights based on case-collapsed cumulative weights in the NO2 air-quality case study. Smaller ESS / n and higher 95th percentile weight values (>> 1) indicate increasingly uneven weighting.
description2: Figure S11 plots effective sample size fraction and upper-tail balancing weights for a NO2 air quality case study, showing uneven weighting with smaller fractions and higher weights.
description3: Two-panel scatter plot displays effective sample size fraction (ESS / n) versus 95th percentile weight for several cases, showing increasing uneven weighting.
questions: How does the balancing of air-quality data relate to effective sample size?
What is the general trend observed in the upper-tail balancing weights?

caption: Figure S12: Illustration of sampling designs and resulting model predictions in the virtual species simulation experiment. Columns correspond to sampling designs (random, clustered, and biased), and rows show the true virtual species distribution (top), predictions from a random forest model (middle), and predictions from a linear model (bottom). Black crosses indicate sample locations.
description2: Figure S12 illustrates sampling designs and resulting model predictions in a virtual species simulation experiment, with columns representing different sampling methods and rows showing true distribution and predicted outcomes.
description3: Four-panel plot displays true distributions, random forest predictions, and linear model predictions for random, clustered, and biased sampling designs, with black crosses marking sample locations.
questions: How do different sampling designs influence the resulting model predictions?
What patterns are visible in the illustration of species distribution predictions?

caption: Figure S13: Mean RMSE estimator error across simulation runs with 95% confidence intervals in the virtual species simulation study. The RMSE estimator error is defined as the difference between the estimated RMSE and the true deployment RMSE.
description2: The figure plots mean Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) estimator errors across simulation runs, accompanied by 95% confidence intervals, in a virtual species simulation study.
description3: Three multi-panel plots display mean RMSE estimator error with 95% confidence intervals for random, clustered, and biased sampling.
questions: What factors influence the magnitude of the RMSE estimator error shown?
How are confidence intervals represented for the RMSE estimator?

caption: Figure S15: Effective sample size fraction (ESS / n) and upper-tail (95th percentile) balancing weights based on case-collapsed cumulative weights in the virtual species simulation study. Smaller ESS / n and higher 95th percentile weight values (>> 1) indicate increasingly uneven weighting.
description2: The figure plots the effective sample size fraction and upper-tail balancing weights based on case-collapsed cumulative weights in a virtual species simulation study.
description3: Two-panel figure displays box plots of effective sample size fraction and 95th percentile weight across three sampling methods and four variables.
questions: What main idea or comparison does the figure present?
What overall structure or pattern is shown in the figure?

caption: Figure S14: Standard deviation of RMSE estimator error across simulation runs in the virtual species simulation study. The RMSE estimator error is defined as the difference between the estimated RMSE and the true deployment RMSE.
description2: The figure plots the standard deviation of the Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) estimator error across simulation runs in a virtual species simulation study.
description3: The plot presents a multi-panel scatterplot displaying the standard deviation of RMSE estimator error for different methods across three sampling types.
questions: How does the simulation method affect the standard deviation of RMSE estimator error?
What is the range of the RMSE estimator error difference observed in the study?
